system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 6: How to Study the Harmonic Fields

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 6 — How to Study the Harmonic Fields

_Pages 206-241._

## Study Devices for Harmonic Fields

> a) Play the harmonic /ields in the same key until you feel comfortable.
>   b) Always study with rhythm (funk, swing, etc). It helps you to memorize things faster.
>   c) Before you change or add a new key, make sure you know the key you practiced previously.
>   d) To make visualization easier, choose chords that are close to each other, using only the 6th
>   and 5th string chords. The 4th string chords should be studied only after you are comfortable
>   with the others.

**Significance (p. 187).** Lays out the four-rule practice protocol that governs how the entire harmonic field chapter must be drilled.

## Visualization via bass note

> The best way to not get lost on the guitar fretboard, is to 5irst visualize the bass note of the
>   chord. However, different from scales, we must visualize the root of the chord (bass note)
>   only using the 5th and 6th strings.

**Significance (p. 187).** Defines the chord-root visualization rule that distinguishes chord study from scale study on the fretboard.

## Ist degree: C6 vs Cmaj7

> We can replace C6 by Cmaj7 or vice-versa almost all the time. The only exception is when
> the melody is the octave. In this case we must use C6, in order to not create a con<lict
> between the melody and the chord (a b9 interval). An important thing to know is that we
> rarely use the perfect 4th over an Ionian chord. However we have some exceptions, where
> we can <ind chords with 4ths and 3rds or even omitted 3rd chords.

**Significance (p. 200).** States the substitution rule and the b9-conflict exception governing the Ionian (I) chord choices.

## IIm degree dorian replacements

> We can replace Dm7(9) dorian by Dm7(11) or even Dm7(13). In theory, we could also use
> Dm6, but minor 6th chords are more complex than they look, so we will come back to them
> later. For now, let’s consider that they can sometimes replace a minor 7th chord.

**Significance (p. 204).** Establishes which extended dorian voicings are interchangeable on the II degree and flags m6 as a deferred topic.

## IIIm degree phrygian options

> We can often add a +lat 6th in the IIIm7 degree. Example: Em7 can be replaced by Em7(b6),
>   Cmaj7/E or even C/E, which are the same chords. However, as a phrygian chord, Em7 can
>   also be replaced by Esus(b9) and sometimes Em7(b9), which was often used by composers
>   such as Antonio Carlos Jobim and also Heitor Villa Lobos.

**Significance (p. 207).** Names the b6 addition and slash-chord equivalences for the III degree and the phrygian sus(b9) substitution.

## IV degree lydian #4 rule

> We do not have to put the (#4) in the chord all the time, but in a few cases it might work. In
>  theory, G/F can be considered an F Lydian chord, including G7(13) or G7(9).

**Significance (p. 209).** Clarifies that the lydian #4 is optional, and identifies slash voicings that imply F lydian.

## V degree dominant sus substitution

> We can replace the V chord (dominant) with a sus chord. Example: G7 can be replaced by
>   Gsus9 or Gsus13. In theory, every Dm/G can be considered as a mixolydian chord even
>   without the note B, which is the 3rd of G.

**Significance (p. 213).** Defines the V-to-sus substitution and the Dm/G mixolydian reinterpretation, central to dominant function harmony.

## VIm degree aeolian options

> We can replace Am7(9) Aeolian with Am7(11) or even Am7(b6). But just like any degree,
>   the characteristic notes do not have to be in the chord all the time. In theory, every Dm7/A
>   or Fmaj7/A can be considered as an Aeolian chord.

**Significance (p. 217).** Lists the extended aeolian replacements and slash-chord aeolian voicings for the VI degree.

## VIIm locrian avoid-note rule

> Bø can be replaced with G7/B or even G/B. This happens frequently because we consider
>   the Bø as being G7(9)/B. Another important thing is that the VII degree cannot use the 9th
>   in the chord, because it would be ?lat 9th, which does not work properly for this type of
>   chord. Remember that the ?lat 9th is an avoid note on locrian chord.

**Significance (p. 221).** Captures the locrian substitution to G7/B and the b9 avoid-note rule that constrains VII-degree voicings.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
